name: let-chains-advisor
description: Identifies deeply nested if-let expressions and suggests let chains for cleaner control flow. Activates when users write nested conditionals with pattern matching.
capabilities: Detects nested if-let expressions at 3+ levels of nesting
Suggests let chain refactoring for cleaner control flow
Identifies multiple Option and Result unwrapping patterns
Recognizes pattern matching with guard conditions
Provides before/after code examples for refactoring
Verifies Rust version compatibility for let chains
author: EmilLindfors
securitySummary: This is a documentation-only skill containing Rust code examples. All static findings are false positives: Markdown code block delimiters were misidentified as shell backticks, and YAML frontmatter was misclassified as cryptographic code. No executable code, network calls, or file system access exists.
valueStatement: Developers writing deeply nested Rust control flow struggle with readability and maintainability. This skill proactively identifies nested if-let patterns (3+ levels) and suggests let chain refactorings for cleaner, more readable Rust 2024 code.
userTitle: Refactor nested if-let with let chains
limitations: Only activates for Rust code, not other languages
Does not auto-apply refactorings, only suggests them
Requires Rust 1.88+ or Rust 2024 edition for let chains
Does not analyze runtime performance impact of refactoring

useCases.targetUser: Rust developers working with optional values
useCases.title: Refactor deeply nested Option handling
useCases.description: A developer has multiple levels of if-let Some() checks for Option types. The skill suggests consolidating them into a single let chain for improved readability.

useCases.title: Simplify Result error handling
useCases.description: When chaining Result checks with if let Ok(), the skill recommends let chain syntax to flatten nested error handling logic.

useCases.title: Clean up complex guard clauses
useCases.description: Developers writing pattern matches with multiple conditions receive suggestions to use let chains with && conditions for better structure.

promptTemplates.title: Basic nested pattern detection
promptTemplates.prompt: I have this Rust code with nested if-let statements. Can you suggest a let chain refactoring for it?
promptTemplates.scenario: User shows nested if-let code and wants refactoring help

promptTemplates.title: Option chaining refactoring
promptTemplates.prompt: How can I rewrite this deeply nested Option unwrapping using let chains in Rust 2024?
promptTemplates.scenario: User specifically asks about let chain syntax for Options

promptTemplates.title: Result and Option mix
promptTemplates.prompt: I need to handle both Option and Result types in my control flow. Show me how let chains can simplify this nested structure.
promptTemplates.scenario: User has mixed error types and wants cleaner syntax

promptTemplates.title: While loop let chains
promptTemplates.prompt: Can I use let chains inside a while let loop? Show me how to refactor this pattern.
promptTemplates.scenario: User wants to apply let chains in iterator processing

bestPractices: Activate the skill when you see 3+ levels of nested if-let statements
Suggest let chains when all branches follow the same return/continue pattern
Verify the Rust version is 1.88+ or edition 2024 before recommending let chains
Provide both the original nested code and the refactored let chain version
antiPatterns: Leaving deeply nested if-let patterns without suggesting refactoring
Applying let chains when branches have different logic (use match instead)
Forgetting that let chains require Rust 1.88 or later
Using let chains for just 2 levels when nested if-let is equally readable

faq.question: What are let chains in Rust?
faq.answer: Let chains combine multiple pattern matches and conditions using && in a single if let statement. They were stabilized in Rust 1.88 and are the preferred way to handle deeply nested pattern matching.

faq.question: When should I suggest let chains?
faq.answer: Suggest let chains when you see 3+ levels of nested if-let patterns that all follow the same return or continue pattern. They improve readability and reduce indentation.

faq.question: What Rust version is required for let chains?
faq.answer: Let chains require Rust 1.88 or later, or the Rust 2024 edition. Check the project Cargo.toml for edition = 2024 or rust-version = 1.88.

faq.question: Can let chains mix Options and Results?
faq.answer: Yes, let chains can combine if let Some() and if let Ok() patterns, as well as add boolean conditions with && operators. This makes handling mixed error types cleaner.

faq.question: When should I NOT use let chains?
faq.answer: Avoid let chains when branches contain different logic. In those cases, a match expression or early return pattern is clearer. Also, for just 2 levels of nesting, the benefit is minimal.

faq.question: Do let chains affect runtime performance?
faq.answer: No, let chains compile to the same code as nested if-let statements. The benefit is purely in code readability and maintainability during development.
